ordering_note: KEY ORDERING TRAP (inverse of scenario 9). Rules evaluate in LOAD ORDER, first match wins (f3 section 2.3). The always rule is listed FIRST, so uid=0 execve matches 'always' and is RECORDED before the never rule (line 2) is consulted. The never rule is therefore INERT for the execve traffic the always rule already catches -- it does NOT suppress. The full always volume stands (no reduction). A model that treats ANY never rule as suppressive regardless of position WRONGLY subtracts volume here.
effective_order: -a never,exit -S execve -F 'uid=0'  (SECOND; never reached for the matched traffic; INERT)

rules.note: FIRST in load order; records ALL execve including uid=0. Full HIGH volume stands; the later never rule does NOT reduce it.

INERT: placed AFTER the always rule, so first-match-wins means the always already recorded uid=0 execve before this never is consulted. It SUPPRESSES NOTHING here (it would only matter for syscalls the always rule does not catch first). direction=inert (NOT suppressive; the never is dead for this traffic). Contrast scenario 9 (never ABOVE always => suppressive).

dominant_tier: HIGH (additive); the trailing never is INERT (suppresses nothing)
rate_band_basis: f3 section 6: always execve HIGH, FULL band (no suppression). The trailing never contributes 0 AND removes nothing because the always matched first.
